question: With reference to Madanapalle of Andhra Pradesh, which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Pingali Venkayya designed the tricolour Indian National Flag here.
Pattabhi Sitaramaiah led the Quit India Movement of Andhra region from here.
Rabindranath Tagore translated the National Anthem from Bengali to English here.
Madame Blavatsky and Colonel Olcott set up headquarters of Theosophical Society first here.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

Option c is correct: The National anthem was first sung outside Calcutta by Rabindranath Tagore himself during a session at the Besant Theosophical College in Madanapalle, Andhra Pradesh, on 28th Feb 1919, when Tagore went to college and sang the song.

Then deputy director of the university (also an expert in European music and wife of the Irish poet James Cousins), asked Tagore to create an English translation of the song and establish the musical notation.

Tagore then translated the Bengali song to English and the tune was composed by Margaret with the girls in the college singing the chorus.

question:
With reference to the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act of 1919, consider the following statements:
Statement I: It was enacted on the recommendation of the Strachey Commission.
Statement II: It allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years.
Statement III: It was repealed as a consequence of the Gandhi-Irwin Pact of 1931.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

Statement I is incorrect: The Rowlatt Act was officially called the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act 1919. It was based on the recommendations of the Rowlatt Commission (Sedition Committee), headed by the British judge, Sir Sidney Rowlatt. The Richard Strachey Commission was created to develop a general strategy and principles to deal with the famines in India.

Statement II is correct: The Act essentially sought to criminalize dissent, empowered the government to suppress political activities, and allowed the detention of political prisoners without trial or judicial review for two years. Mahatma Gandhi referred to it as a “Black Law”.

Statement III is incorrect: In 1919, Gandhiji launched a nationwide protest against the Rowlatt Act. The protests spread throughout India, and the British authorities were eventually forced to repeal the Rowlatt Act in 1922.

question:
In the context of Jallianwala Bagh Massacre during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following statements:
Statement I: Mahatma Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind, bestowed by the British for his work during the Boer War in protest against the massacre.
Statement II: The Hunter Committee, which was set up to investigate the Massacre, did not have any Indian as members.
Statement III: Sardar Udham Singh assassinated Brigadier General Reginald Dyer who ordered the opening of fire on people in Jallianwala Bagh.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

The Jallianwala Bagh massacre took place on April 13, 1919 in Amritsar, Punjab. British troops under the command of General Reginald Dyer opened fire on a large gathering of Indians.

Statement I is correct: Mahatma Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind in protest of the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre. The title was bestowed by the British for his work during the Boer War. (Note: Rabindranath Tagore renounced his knighthood).

Statement II is incorrect: The Disorders Inquiry Committee (Hunter Commission) had three Indian members namely Sir Chimanlal Harilal Setalvad, Pandit Jagat Narayan and Sardar Sahibzada Sultan Ahmad Khan.

Statement III is incorrect: Brigadier-General Reginald Dyer ordered troops to launch indiscriminate firing. Lieutenant Governor of Punjab Michael O’Dwyer imposed martial rule and sent Dyer. Michael O’Dwyer was assassinated in 1940 by Sardar Udham Singh, who was present at Jallianwala Bagh.

question:
With reference to the movements/protests associated with Mahatma Gandhi in India, consider the following information:

Movement/Protest — Primary Objective of the Movement/Protest — Leader Associated
I. Champaran Satyagraha — Abolition of Tinkathia system of indigo cultivation — Rajendra Prasad
II. Kheda Satyagraha — Remission of taxes following the failure of the agricultural harvest that year — Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
III. Ahmedabad Mill Strike — Reduction in export duties imposed on Indian textiles — Ambalal Sarabhai

In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Row I is correct: Champaran Satyagraha of 1917 was launched against the Tinkathia system of indigo cultivation. Gandhi was joined by Rajendra Prasad, Mazhar-ul-Haq, Mahadeo Desai, Narhari Parekh, and J.B. Kripalani.

Row II is correct: The Kheda Satyagraha of 1918 was organised to protest against the oppressive tax imposed on peasants despite them having suffered huge losses due to crop failure in 1917-18. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel played an important role in organising the villagers.

Row III is incorrect: Ahmedabad Mill Strike of 1918 was a hunger strike initiated by Gandhi in support of the mill workers over the issue of discontinuation of the plague bonus and a demand for a 35 percent wage hike, not export duties. Ambalal Sarabhai was a mill owner, and his sister Anusuya Sarabhai helped the workers.

question:
Which among the following statements about Mahatma Gandhi’s Satyagraha is/are correct?
Statement 1: It was during the course of his campaign against racialism in South Africa that Gandhiji first applied Satyagraha.
Statement 2: The two vital ingredients of Satyagraha are ‘truth’ and ‘non-violence’.
Statement 3: The Satyagrahi resists evil by inflicting suffering on himself and not by inflicting suffering on the opponent.
Statement 4: In India, Satyagraha was first tried by Gandhiji in Champaran.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Statement 1 is correct: Mahatma Gandhi developed and employed the concept of Satyagraha during his years in South Africa while leading the Indian community in their struggle against racial discrimination.

Statement 2 is correct: Gandhiji emphasized that Satyagraha is based on the principles of truth (Satya) and nonviolence (Ahimsa).

Statement 3 is correct: In Satyagraha, the emphasis is on self-suffering and self-restraint rather than causing harm or suffering to the opponent.

Statement 4 is correct: One of the early instances of Gandhiji’s application of Satyagraha in India was during the Champaran Satyagraha in 1917.

question:
With reference to Swaraj Party in the Indian Freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
Statement I: C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru were leaders associated with the Party.
Statement II: The members of the party advocated for participating in the elections to the legislative councils to expose the hollowness of British rule in India from within these councils.
Statement III: It introduced the Public Safety Bill (1928) in the Central Legislative Assembly to safeguard civil liberties.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Statement I is correct: C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru formed the Swaraj Party within the Congress to argue for a return to council politics.

Statement II is correct: The members of the Swaraj Party advocated for participating in the elections to the Legislative Councils to expose the hollowness of British rule from within and use them as an arena of political struggle.

Statement III is incorrect: One of their noteworthy achievements was the defeat of the Public Safety Bill in 1928, which was aimed at empowering the Government to deport undesirable and subversive foreigners (mainly socialists and communists). They did not introduce it to safeguard liberties, but rather defeated it to protect activists.

question:
Consider the following events:
I. Appointment of Nehru Committee to draft a Constitution for India.
II. Establishment of the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI).
III. Formation of Hindustan Republic Association.

Which of the following is the correct chronological sequence of the above events?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

The correct chronological sequence is III - II - I.

Formation of Hindustan Republic Association (1924): Founded in October 1924 at Kanpur, aiming to overthrow colonial rule through armed struggle.

Establishment of FICCI (1927): Indian merchants and industrialists formed the Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI) in 1927 to organise business interests and protect against imports.

Appointment of Nehru Committee (1928): Appointed at an All Parties Conference in February 1928 to draft a Constitution for India. The report was finalised by August 1928.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question:
Before launching the Civil Disobedience movement in India, Mahatma Gandhi served a memorandum with certain demands of the people to the Viceroy Lord Irwin. In this context consider the following demands:
I. Acceptance of Purna Swaraj as the constitutional status of India
II. Removal of land revenue as a subject from the legislative control
III. Abolition of the salt tax
IV. Introduction of provincial autonomy under the federal structure
V. Abolition of the Criminal Investigation Department (CID)

How many of the above were part of the memorandum served by Gandhiji to the Viceroy?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Mahatma Gandhi presented 11 demands to Viceroy Lord Irwin on January 31, 1930.

Option I is incorrect: Purna Swaraj was the objective set at the Lahore Session of 1929, but the 11-point memorandum did not mention the resolve to attain Purna Swaraj.

Option II is incorrect: He demanded that land revenue should be reduced by half (50%) and made a subject of legislative control, not taken out of their control.

Option III is correct: Salt tax should be abolished and also the government monopoly over manufacturing of salt.

Option IV is incorrect: Introduction of provincial autonomy under the federal structure was not one of the eleven demands.

Option V is correct: Abolition of the Criminal Investigation Department (C.I.D.) was one of the administrative demands.

question:
In the context of the Lahore session of the Indian National Congress, 1929 consider the following statements:
Statement I: The resolution for Poorn Swaraj (Complete Independence) of India was unanimously adopted at this session.
Statement II: A resolution in this session was unanimously adopted rejecting the two-nation theory.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Statement I is correct: At the end of December 1929, the INC held its annual session in Lahore. Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru drafted a resolution focusing on the objective of attaining complete independence (Purna Swaraj), which was unanimously passed.

Statement II is incorrect: The formal beginning of the Two-Nation Theory came up in March 1940 when the Muslim League passed the Lahore Resolution. Since the INC Session of Lahore happened in 1929-30, it could not have passed a resolution rejecting a theory that was not yet formally proposed.

question: With reference to History of Modern India, the book ‘Folklore of Southern India’ was written by:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Option d is correct: In late-nineteenth-century India, nationalists began recording folk tales and songs to preserve folk tradition and restore national pride. In Madras, Natesa Sastri published a massive four-volume collection of Tamil folk tales titled The Folklore of Southern India. He believed that folklore was national literature and the most trustworthy manifestation of people’s real thoughts and characteristics.

question: Mahatma Gandhi said that some of his deepest convictions were reflected in a book titled, “Unto this Last’’ and the book transformed his life. What was the message from the book that transformed Mahatma Gandhi?
options: Uplifting the oppressed and poor is the moral responsibility of an educated man
The good of individual is contained in the good of all
The life of celibacy and spiritual pursuit are essential for a noble life
All the statements (a), (b) and (c) are correct in this context
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Option b is correct: Gandhi translated John Ruskin's Unto This Last into Gujarati in 1908 under the title Sarvodaya (Well Being of All). He summarised its teachings into three points:
1. That the good of the individual is contained in the good of all.
2. That a lawyer’s work has the same value as the barber’s, as all have the same right of earning their livelihood from their work.
3. That a life of labour, i.e., the life of the tiller of the soil and the handicraftsman, is the life worth living.

question: What was the primary objective behind the establishment of Praja Mandals during the Indian freedom movement?
options: To function as parallel governments in the rural areas of British Indian provinces.
To spread revolutionary nationalism through secret societies in both British Indian provinces as well as princely states.
To organize armed peasant uprisings against oppressive land revenue systems in the British Indian provinces.
To promote the development of democratic and national consciousness in princely states.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Option d is correct: Praja Mandals (also called Lok Parishads) were political bodies formed by the people of princely states during the Indian freedom struggle, mainly from the 1910s–1920s onward.

The primary objective behind establishing these Praja Mandals was to fight against the autocratic and feudal rule of the princes and demand democratic reforms and responsible government within the princely states. They also fought for civil liberties and an end to oppressive practices like forced labor (begar).

question:
In the context of History of Modern India, consider the following:
I. She is considered as the first Indian woman to contest an election to a legislative body in 1926.
II. She actively participated in the Salt Satyagraha.
III. Post Independence, she played an important role in the establishment of national bodies such as the All-India Handicrafts Board and National School of Drama.

Which of the following Indian women leaders is best described above?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Option b is correct: Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay (1903–1988) created history by becoming the first Indian woman to contest a legislative election in 1926, standing for the Madras Legislative Council.

During the freedom struggle, she actively participated in the Salt Satyagraha (1930) and was among the earliest women to be arrested in the Civil Disobedience Movement.

After independence, she emerged as a key architect of India’s cultural and handicrafts policy. She was closely associated with the establishment of institutions such as the All-India Handicrafts Board, Central Cottage Industries Emporium, National School of Drama, and Sangeet Natak Academy.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question:
With reference to the Second Round Table Conference (1931) held in London during the freedom struggle of India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
Statement I: It was held during the tenure of Lord Willingdon as the Viceroy of India.
Statement II: Mahatma Gandhi participated in the Conference as the sole representative of the Indian National Congress.
Statement III: The success of the Second Round Table Conference in attaining its desired objectives resulted in the suspension of the Civil Disobedience Movement in India.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Statement I is correct: The Second Round Table Conference was held from September to December 1931. At this time, Lord Willingdon had assumed office as the Viceroy of India (1931–1936), succeeding Lord Irwin.

Statement II is correct: Mahatma Gandhi participated in the Conference as the sole representative of the Indian National Congress, following the Gandhi–Irwin Pact (1931).

Statement III is incorrect: The Civil Disobedience Movement was suspended before the Conference as a consequence of the Gandhi–Irwin Pact. The Conference itself failed to reach a consensus on key constitutional and political issues, and after Gandhi's return, the Civil Disobedience Movement was resumed, not suspended.

question: What was the Dutt-Bradley Thesis?
options: The Working Committee of the Indian National Congress decided that Congress should play a crucial role in realising the independence of India.
The Socialist Party decided to play the foremost part in the anti-imperialist struggle.
Revolutionary socialist Batukeshwar Dutt put forth a ten-point plan to work for the success of the anti-imperialist front.
It was a Communist Party document, according to which the National Congress could play a great part and a foremost part in realising the anti-imperialist people’s front.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Option d is correct: The Dutt-Bradley Thesis was a document written by Rajni Palme Dutt and Ben Bradley, two leaders of the Communist Party, published in February 1936. The thesis argued that the Indian National Congress could play a great part and a foremost part in realizing the anti-imperialist people’s front in India.

question:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Pravasi Bharatiya Diwas is celebrated by the Government of India once every two years on 9 January.
Statement II: Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa to India on Jan 9, 1915.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Statement I is correct: Pravasi Bharatiya Divas is celebrated once in every two years on 9th January to strengthen the engagement of the overseas Indian community with the Government of India and reconnect them with their cultural roots.

Statement II is correct: Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa to India on 9 January 1915. He had gone there in 1893 and organised the Indian workers against racial discrimination using Satyagraha.

Conclusion: 9 January is observed as Pravasi Bharatiya Divas specifically to commemorate the return of Mahatma Gandhi from South Africa to India in 1915. Thus, Statement II correctly explains Statement I.

question: In the context of the Indian freedom struggle, who among the following was elected as the President of the first All India Khilafat Conference held at Delhi in 1919?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Option d is correct: The Khilafat Movement in India emerged to protect the institution of the Khalifa (Caliph) in Turkey after the First World War. The All India Khilafat Conference was held in Delhi in November 1919, and Mahatma Gandhi was elected as its President. The conference passed important resolutions advocating the boycott of peace celebrations, non-cooperation with the government, and the boycott of British goods.

question:
Consider the following:
I. Appointment of a Congress working committee (CWC) of 15 members.
II. Abolition of any entry fee for joining the Indian National Congress.
III. Reorganisation of Provincial Congress Committees (PCCs) on a linguistic basis.
IV. Opening up of Congress membership to all men and women of the age of 21 or above.

Which of the above reforms were adopted at the Nagpur Session of the Indian National Congress (1920)?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

The annual session of the INC was held in December 1920 in Nagpur, where organizational changes were made to enable its functioning as a continuous political organization.

Option I is correct: The appointment of a fifteen-member Congress Working Committee to guide the Congress was a significant outcome.

Option II is incorrect: The entry fee for Congress membership was reduced to four annas, not abolished completely.

Option III is correct: Provincial Congress Committees were reorganised on a linguistic basis, and ward-level committees were also established.

Option IV is correct: Membership was opened to all men and women above the age of 21 (which was further reduced to 18 years in 1921).

question:
Before launching a full scale civil disobedience campaign as one of the stages of the Non Cooperation movement, Mahatma Gandhi suspended the movement in 1922. What is/are the possible reasons behind the suspension of the movement?
I. The resolution for full scale civil disobedience campaign was rejected by the Indian National Congress as at the time Congress’s goal was to secure Swaraj (self-government) through constitutional means.
II. Violence in Bombay following the Prince of Wales’ visit to India convinced Gandhiji that masses have not understood the message of Non-Violence.
III. Violence in Chauri Chaura, where a number of people were injured and killed.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Gandhiji proposed that the Non Cooperation movement should unfold in stages. It should begin with the surrender of titles that the government awarded, and a boycott of civil services, army, police, courts and legislative councils, schools, and foreign goods. Then, in case the government used repression, a full civil disobedience campaign would be launched.

Statement I is incorrect: The All-India Congress Committee did not reject Civil Disobedience in principle. In July 1921, the Congress authorised local Congress committees to initiate Civil Disobedience if it was thought that the people were ready for it. In the Indian National Congress Session of Nagpur the Congress’s previous goal to secure Swaraj (self-government) through constitutional means was changed. Now the Congress decided to attain Swaraj through peaceful and legitimate means.

Statement II is correct: The visit of Prince of Wales to India in November 1921 triggered one of the major violent outbreaks of the movement, with attacks on Europeans, Anglo-Indians, and Parsis on the streets of Bombay. These incidents convinced Gandhi that the masses were not yet ready to adhere to strict non-violence.

Statement III is correct: In the Chauri-Chaura incident on February 5, 1922, twenty-two policemen were killed in the violence, prompting Gandhiji to withdraw the Non-cooperation movement. This was the immediate reason behind the suspension of the movement.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question:
With reference to the History of Modern India, which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Poona Pact, 1932?
I. It was signed between the Indian National Congress and the Viceroy on behalf of the British Prime Minister.
II. It provided for reservation of seats for the Depressed classes in central as well as provincial legislatures.
III. Under the Pact, the Congress agreed to participate in the Second Round Table Conference.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

On September 20, 1932, Mahatma Gandhi began a fast unto death in Pune’s Yerawada Jail in protest against the award of separate electorates for Scheduled Castes.

Statement I is incorrect: On 24th September, 1932 B.R. Ambedkar signed the Poona Pact with Madan Mohan Malviya, who represented Mahatma Gandhi. The pact replaced the separate electorate with reservations for the Depressed Classes.

Statement II is correct: The Poona Pact contained reservation of seats for the Depressed Classes within the frame of joint electorate. 148 seats were reserved for the Depressed Classes in the provincial legislatures. In the Central legislature, 18 per cent of the seats were reserved for the Depressed Classes. Election to these seats was by joint electorates.

Statement III is incorrect: The Poona Pact was signed on 24th September 1932. The second Round Table Conference had already been held in London from 7 September 1931 to 1 December 1931.

question:
With reference to the British colonial rule in India, consider the following statements:
1. Mahatma Gandhi was instrumental in the abolition of the system of ‘indentured labour’.
2. In Lord Chelmsford’s ‘War Conference’, Mahatma Gandhi did not support the resolution on recruiting Indians for World War.
3. Consequent upon the breaking of Salt Law by Indian people, the Indian National Congress was declared illegal by the colonial rulers.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Statement 1 is correct: Indian immigration to South Africa had begun in 1890 when White settlers recruited indentured Indian labour, mainly from South India, to work on sugar plantations. During the early 1900s, Gandhi was instrumental in getting the indentured labour system abolished in the British Empire through the use of passive resistance or civil disobedience, named Satyagraha.

Statement 2 is incorrect: The Viceroy of India Lord Chelmsford invited various Indian leaders to attend a War conference during World War I. After meeting the Viceroy, Gandhi supported the government’s resolution on recruiting Indians for the World War. He believed that whole-hearted co-operation with the government would bring India within sight of the goal of Swaraj.

Statement 3 is correct: Gandhiji began his salt march from Ahmedabad to Dandi on 12th March 1930. The British government took many leaders under custody. In dealing with the breakers of the salt law, the police resorted to their brutal methods and The Indian National Congress was declared illegal.

question:
In the context of modern Indian history, which of the following is/are the recommendations of the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) Report?
I. Continuation of Dyarchy in the Provinces.
II. Establishment of Responsible Government at the Centre alongwith Viceroy being accountable to the Central legislature.
III. Separation of Burma from British India.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

The British government established the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) to assess India's readiness for constitutional reforms. The Commission published its report in May 1930.

Statement I is incorrect: It recommended the scrapping of Dyarchy in the provinces and the establishment of responsible unitary government in provinces.

Statement II is incorrect: The Report declared that the establishment of a responsible government at the centre was to wait indefinitely. It recommended a Greater India consisting of British India and the Princely States as a federal association, but the clause of British Paramountcy (with Viceroy as the agent of Paramount power) was to remain.

Statement III is correct: The Simon Commission report suggested the separation of Sindh from Bombay province. Besides, it also recommended the separation of Burma (Myanmar) from British India as it was not a natural part of India.

question: With reference to British Colonial rule in India, the Inland Emigration Act of 1859 was primarily enacted with which one of the following objectives?
options: To prevent the plantation workers from leaving tea gardens without permission.
To abolish the exploitative system of indentured labour.
To facilitate the supply of Indian indentured labour to other British colonies.
To prevent illegal migration of Indians to other British colonies.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

The Inland Emigration Act of 1859 prohibited the plantation workers in India from leaving the tea gardens without permission. The Act stipulated that the plantation workers could leave the tea gardens only after the permission of the plantation owners.

The Act effectively bound the workers to the tea gardens and severely restricted the freedom of movement of plantation workers in India. It was primarily aimed at securing a steady supply of labor for the rapidly expanding tea industry in Assam and other regions by preventing workers from leaving the plantation sites.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: During the freedom struggle of India, a formal resolution was passed in a session of Indian National Congress (INC) adopting Pingali Vekaiah's/Venkayya’s designed flag as India’s flag with a little modification. While the white and green remained, the red was replaced by saffron. In this context, in which of the following sessions of INC was such a resolution passed?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

The idea of a national flag for India evolved gradually during the freedom struggle.

In 1921, at the Bezwada (now Vijayawada) session of Congress, Pingali Venkayya presented a design of a flag to Mahatma Gandhi. The flag consisted of three stripes representing multiple communities with a spinning wheel superimposed in the center.

In 1931, at the Karachi Session, a formal resolution was passed adopting Pingali Venkayya’s flag with a little modification. While the white and green remained, the red was replaced by saffron. The saffron was for courage, white for peace, and green for fertility and growth.

question: Who among the following founded the Bhil Seva Mandal in 1922?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Amritlal Vithaldas Thakkar, popularly known as Thakkar Bapa, was an Indian social worker who worked for the upliftment of tribal people in Gujarat state in India.

He became a member of the Servants of India Society founded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale in 1905. In 1922, he founded the Bhil Seva Mandal. Later, he became the general secretary of the Harijan Sevak Sangh founded by Mahatma Gandhi in 1932.

question:
With reference to the Mahatma Gandhi’s works/activities in South Africa, consider the following statements:
I. He set up the Natal Indian Congress to safeguard the political rights of Indians in Natal.
II. He started the newspaper Indian Opinion to mobilize public opinion against racial discrimination.
III. He established the Phoenix Settlement and Tolstoy Farm as centers for community living and satyagraha training.
IV. He founded the All-India Spinners’ Association during his stay in South Africa to promote khadi among the Indian diaspora.

Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Mahatma Gandhi stayed in South Africa from 1893 to 1914, a period that proved to be a turning point in his life and political philosophy.

Statement I is correct: Mahatma Gandhi established the Natal Indian Congress (NIC) in 1894 in South Africa to organize Indians politically and to protect their civil and political rights against discriminatory laws.

Statement II is correct: He started the weekly newspaper Indian Opinion in 1903 in South Africa to create awareness among Indians and the wider public about racial discrimination and the principles of truth and non-violence.

Statement III is correct: He founded the Phoenix Settlement in 1904 near Durban (Natal). Later, in 1910, he established Tolstoy Farm near Johannesburg. Both settlements functioned as experiments in community living and served as practical training centres for satyagraha.

Statement IV is incorrect: The All-India Spinners’ Association was founded by Mahatma Gandhi in 1925 in India, long after his return from South Africa.

question: In the context of the Indian national movement, who came to be known as the “No-Changers” within the Congress after the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement? The Leaders of the Congress:
options: who opposed council entry and insisted on continuing with the grassroot constructive programmes.
who demanded an end to the policy of non-violence and advocated revolutionary activities.
who believed in the cooperation with the British administration to secure constitutional reforms.
who believed that mass civil disobedience should be resumed immediately without any preparatory work.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

After the withdrawal of the Non-Cooperation Movement in 1922 following the Chauri Chaura incident, the Indian National Congress faced a serious internal debate. This led to the emergence of two distinct groups:

1. No-Changers: The No-Changers opposed entry into the legislative councils and argued that council participation would legitimize colonial rule and dilute the spirit of non-cooperation. Instead, they emphasized constructive programmes as the primary method of struggle, which included the promotion of khadi, development of national education, and social reforms like the removal of untouchability. Leading proponents included C. Rajagopalachari, Vallabhbhai Patel, Rajendra Prasad and MA Ansari.

2. Pro-Changers (Swarajists): The Swarajists wanted the constructive programme to be coupled with a political programme of council-entry (e.g., C.R. Das, Motilal Nehru).

question:
With reference to the Indian freedom struggle, consider the following incidents:
I. Assasination of British Police officer John Saunders
II. Meerut Conspiracy Case
III. Assassination of Curzon-Wyllie, a British official
IV. Assassination of Michael O’Dwyer, a British officer

In how many of the above incidents was Bhagat Singh actively involved?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Incident I is correct: In December 1928, Bhagat Singh and Rajguru shot dead a 21-year-old British police officer, John P Saunders, in Lahore, in a case of mistaken identity while planning to kill James Scott for his role in the death of Lala Lajpat Rai.

Incident II is incorrect: The Meerut Conspiracy case (1929) involved a series of conspiracy cases against Communists. Bhagat Singh was not an accused or participant in this case.

Incident III is incorrect: The assassination of Curzon-Wyllie took place in 1909 in London and was carried out by Madan Lal Dhingra, much before Bhagat Singh emerged as a revolutionary figure.

Incident IV is incorrect: The assassination of Michael O’Dwyer occurred in 1940, long after Bhagat Singh had been executed. It was carried out by Sardar Udham Singh.

question: The ‘All- Bengal Students Conference’ in September 1928 was presided over by:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Students all over the subcontinent played a significant and active role in the Indian Independence Movement. The All-Bengal Students Association was formed in 1928, and the office bearers were students studying in Kolkata district.

After several meetings, its final shape was given during a four-day conference held in September 1928. This ‘All- Bengal Students Conference’ was presided over by Jawaharlal Nehru, and thus the All-Bengal Students Association came into existence in 1928.

question: In the context of the Indian freedom struggle, Shanti Ghosh and Bina Das are best known for involvement in which of the following activities?
options: Participating in revolutionary activities like attacks on British officials in Bengal
Organising the anti-Chaukidari tax campaign in Bihar
Leading tribal uprisings against forest laws in eastern India
Mobilising mill workers during the Non-Cooperation Movement
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

The revolutionary phase of the Indian national movement, especially in the 1920s and 1930s, witnessed a notable participation of young men and women. In Bengal, revolutionary activity acquired a distinct character marked by group action, symbolic attacks on colonial authority, and active involvement of women.

Santi Ghosh was a school student from Comilla who, along with Suniti Choudhury, assassinated the British District Magistrate as an act of revolutionary resistance. Bina Das became widely known for her attempt on the life of the Governor of Bengal in 1932, carried out during her college convocation ceremony. Both represent the direct, revolutionary resistance against British officials in Bengal.

question:
Consider the following subjects with regard to Non-Cooperation Programme:
I. Boycott of law-courts and foreign cloth
II. Observance of strict non-violence
III. Retention of titles and honours without using them in public
IV. Establishment of Panchayats for settling disputes

How many of the above were parts of the Non-Cooperation Programme?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

In September 1920, at a special session in Calcutta, the Indian National Congress approved a non-cooperation programme until the Punjab and Khilafat wrongs were removed and swaraj was established.

Statements I, II and IV are correct: The Programme of Non-Cooperation included the boycott of law courts, government schools, colleges, and foreign cloth. It involved the practice of hand-spinning. It was decided to establish and strengthen panchayats for the settlement of disputes, maintain communal amity, and observe strict non-violence.

Statement III is incorrect: The Non-Cooperation Programme involved the explicit renunciation of government titles and honorary positions, not merely the retention of titles and honours without using them in public.

question:
With reference to Indian freedom struggle, consider the following passage:
In 1928-29 he was elected a member of the committee to associate with the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission). He was one of the first legislators in India to introduce a Bill in the Bombay Legislative council for the abolition of serfdom of agricultural tenants.

Which of the following leaders is described in the above passage?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

Dr. B.R. Ambedkar's parliamentary career began when he was nominated as a member of the Bombay Legislative Council in 1926, the membership of which he held till 1934.

In 1928-29, he was elected a member of the committee to associate with the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission). On 17 September 1937, Ambedkar introduced a Bill in the Bombay Legislative Council for the abolition of the Khoti system in Konkan. He was the first legislator in India to introduce a Bill for the abolition of serfdom of agricultural tenants.

question: “He was a sanyasi who worked in Fiji as an indentured labourer. He organised peasants in Awadh and founded the Oudh Kisan Sabha in October 1920.” Who among the following is referred to in the above passage?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Baba Ramchandra was a sanyasi who had been to Fiji as an indentured labourer. He came back in 1917.

Around 1920, he organised peasants in Awadh against talukdars and landlords who demanded exorbitantly high rents and a variety of other cesses. The movement demanded a reduction of revenue, the abolition of begar, and a social boycott of oppressive landlords. Baba Ramchandra, along with Jawaharlal Nehru and a few others, set up the Oudh Kisan Sabha in October 1920.

question:
With reference to Modern Indian history, consider the following:
I. Establishment of Federal constitution with residual powers to the Provinces
II. A joint mixed electorate throughout India for the election to the House of Representatives and the provincial legislatures.
III. The redistribution of provinces to take place on a linguistic basis.
IV. The right to form associations or unions.

How many of the above given were the recommendations of the Nehru Report which was an outline draft of a constitution at the time?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

The Nehru Committee (chaired by Motilal Nehru) submitted its report in August 1928 for drafting a "Swaraj Constitution for India".

Statement I is incorrect: The Nehru Report proposed the establishment of a responsible government at the Centre and in the provinces but did not contain virtually any federal features. A federal constitution with residuary powers vested in the provinces was demanded in Muhammad Ali Jinnah’s Fourteen Points, not the Nehru Report.

Statements II, III and IV are correct: The Nehru Report rejected the system of separate electorates and demanded joint mixed electorates throughout India. It recommended the redistribution of provinces on a linguistic basis subject to financial and administrative considerations. It also emphasized securing 19 Fundamental Rights, including the right to form associations or unions.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: Who among the following leaders led the first batch of Satyagrahis to break the Salt law from Cuttack to Inchudi in Balasore sea-coast in Orissa in April 1930?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

The Civil Disobedience Movement was formally launched by Mahatma Gandhi on 6 April 1930, when he picked up a handful of salt at Dandi.

Option a is correct: Gopabandhu Chaudhuri was a noted Gandhian leader in Odisha. He led the first batch of Satyagrahis from Cuttack to Inchudi in Balasore sea-coast in Orissa. The movement was prominent in coastal areas like Balasore, Cuttack, and Puri. Inchudi is often called the "second Dandi" of India.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: In the context of the Indian national movement, the Delhi Manifesto of 1929 is related to:
options: declaration of Purna Swaraj as the goal of the Indian National Congress.
rejection of the Simon Commission report and announcement of a nationwide boycott.
laying down conditions for India’s participation in the First Round Table Conference.
the different stages of the civil disobedience movement with the timeline.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

The Delhi Manifesto (2 November 1929) was issued by a conference of prominent national leaders in the context of the proposed Round Table Conference. The Manifesto laid down clear preconditions for Indian participation in the First Round Table Conference.

Key points included: immediate acceptance of Dominion Status as the basic principle; majority representation for the Indian National Congress at the round table conference; and general amnesty for political prisoners.

Lord Irwin ultimately rejected the demands in the Manifesto, thus setting the stage for the civil disobedience movement.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question:
The Communal Award, announced by British Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald in 1932, provided for separate electorates to certain specified communities and categories in British India. In this context, consider the following communities/categories:
I. Europeans
II. Indian Christians
III. Depressed Classes
IV. Sikhs

How many of the above were provided with separate electorates under the original Communal Award of 1932?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

The Communal Award was announced in 1932 by the British Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald at the conclusion of the Second Round Table Conference.

The Award was based on the recommendations of the Indian Franchise Committee (Lothian Committee) and aimed at expanding political representation by continuing the British policy of communal and minority-based safeguards.

Under the Communal Award, separate electorates were granted to various groups, including Muslims, Sikhs, Anglo-Indians, Europeans, Indian Christians, and the Depressed Classes. In a limited manner, even Marathas were accorded a few separate seats in the Bombay Presidency.

Thus, all four categories listed; Europeans, Indian Christians, Depressed Classes, and Sikhs were provided with separate electorates under the Communal Award.

Poona Pact (1932):
The Poona Pact was an agreement between Mahatma Gandhi and Dr. B.R. Ambedkar on 24 September 1932 concerning the political representation of the Depressed Classes. Under the Poona Pact, the separate electorates for the Depressed Classes were abolished. Instead, they were given reserved seats within the general Hindu electorate.

question:
With reference to Indian freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
Statement I: Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose founded the Indian Independence League.
Statement II: Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose opposed the adoption of Dominion Status as the goal of the Indian National Congress as per the Nehru Report.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Statement I is not correct: In 1942, Rash Behari Bose founded the Indian Independence League (IIL) in Japan, with the goal of organizing and mobilizing Indians for the struggle against British rule. Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose founded the Independence for India League in 1928 which advocated complete severance of the British connection with India, and Nehru became its General Secretary.

Statement II is correct: The Nehru Report failed to accommodate the aspirations of various sections of Indian society. The younger section of the Congress led by Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhash Bose were also angered as they thought that the idea of dominion status in the report had disregarded the progress made by Indian Nationalism.

Thus, opposition to Dominion Status motivated Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhas Chandra Bose for the formation of the Independence for India League in 1928 with the goal of complete independence.

question:
With reference to British colonial administration in India, consider the following commissions/committees:
I. Royal Commission on Agriculture (Linlithgow Commission)
II. Indian States Committee (Butler Committee)
III. Reforms Enquiry Committee (Muddiman Committee)
IV. Royal Commission on the Superior Civil Services in India (Lee Commission)

Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the above given commissions/committees based on the year of their constitution?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Option IV: Lee Commission of 1923 is the Royal Commission on the Superior Civil Services in India. In 1923, the Government of India appointed a commission under the chairmanship of Lord Lee to examine the organisation, service conditions, and recruitment methods of both Europeans and Indians.

Option III: Reforms Enquiry Committee (Muddiman Committee) was constituted in 1924 under the chairmanship of Sir Alexander Muddiman. The Committee was tasked with examining the working of dyarchy under the Government of India Act, 1919.

Option I: A Royal Commission on Agriculture in India (Linlithgow Commission) was constituted in 1926 with Lord Linlithgow as its Chairman to examine and report on the condition of the agricultural and rural economy in India.

Option II: Indian States Committee (Butler Committee) was appointed in 1927 under Sir Harcourt Butler to examine the relationship between the princely states and British paramountcy.

Thus, the correct chronological sequence is IV-III-I-II.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: During India’s colonial period, the Cunningham Circular was issued in order to:
options: suppress the participation of students in the anti-partition movement of Bengal.
impose censorship on the vernacular press in India.
prevent the laborers in any kind of strike in tea plantations owned by the Britishers.
prevent the students from participating in the Civil Disobedience Movement.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Option d is correct: When the Civil Disobedience Movement was gaining momentum in the whole country, Assam also played a significant role in contributing to the freedom of the country. People, especially students from the Kamrup district of Assam came forward.

To prevent the students from participating in the Civil Disobedience Movement, J.R. Cunningham, the then Director of Public Information of Assam, came up with the Cunningham circular in 1930.

It required students to sign a declaration pledging their loyalty to the British Raj, renouncing any participation in anti-government activities.

question: Who provided legal defence to the people arrested in the aftermath of the Chauri Chaura incident?
options: Madan Mohan Malaviya and Krishna Kant
Dr. Saifuddin Kitchlew and Khwaja Hasan Nizami
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Madan Mohan Malaviya earned his law degree from Allahabad University in 1891 and practiced law at the Allahabad High Court. He was known as a brilliant Civil Lawyer and was highly respected by his peers.

When he appeared in the High Court in 1923 after an interval of ten years, it was to support the appeal of the 170 persons sentenced to death by the Session judge of Gorakhpur in the Chauri Chaura case.

In this case 225 persons had been tried for setting fire to the police station of Chauri Chaura (in Deoria district) and causing the death of one Sub-Inspector and 21 constables trapped in it.

question:
With reference to the decline of Khilafat movement during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following statements:
Statement I: By the beginning of 1923, the Khilafat issue had largely lost its relevance in India.
Statement II: The Treaty of Sèvres formally abolished the institution of the Caliphate in Turkey.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II does not explain Statement I
Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

Statement I is correct: The Non-Cooperation Movement was withdrawn after the Chauri Chaura incident in 1922. Subsequently, the Khilafat issue also lost its relevance when Kemal Pasha came to power in Turkey in 1923. The Sultan of Turkey was stripped of all political power. Kemal Pasha wanted to modernize Turkey and to make it a secular state. The Caliphate was abolished. Naturally it led to an end of Khilafat movement.

Statement II is incorrect: The Treaty of Sèvres, signed in May 1920, imposed harsh peace terms on Turkey and led to the extensive dismemberment of its territories. However it did not abolish the institution of the Caliphate. The Caliphate was formally abolished later, in 1924.

question:
With reference to Mahatma Gandhi’s initiatives for the welfare of the Depressed Classes, consider the following organisations/bodies:
I. All India Scheduled Castes Federation
II. Independent Labour Party
III. Bahishkrit Hitakarini Sabha
IV. Harijan Sewak Sangh

How many of the above organisations/bodies were founded by Mahatma Gandhi?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Option I is incorrect: All India Scheduled Caste Federation was founded by Dr B.R. Ambedkar in 1942. The Federation was designed to be a national-level political party dedicated to the interests of the Scheduled Castes.

Option II is incorrect: On August 15, 1936, Dr B. R. Ambedkar formed the Independent Labour Party to safeguard the interest of the depressed classes, which mostly formed the labour population.

Option III is incorrect: Dr B.R. Ambedkar established the Bahishkrit Hitakarini Sabha in 1924 to highlight the difficulties and grievances of the dalits before the government. Its motto was: ‘Educate, Agitate and Organise’.

Option IV is correct: Mahatma Gandhi set up the All India Anti-Untouchability League in September 1932 as part of his efforts to eradicate the concept of ‘untouchability’ from India’s caste system. He put his close friend, the pioneering industrialist Ghanshyam Das Birla, in charge of the organisation. It was also called the Harijan Sewak Sangh.

question: In the 1927 Madras session of Indian National Congress, a resolution was passed calling for the boycott of the Simon Commission. Who among the following presided over the 1927 Madras session of INC?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

Option a is correct: In 1927, The Indian National Congress held its session (December 26th to 28th) in Madras district (present-day Chennai), Tamil Nadu. Dr Mukhtar Ahmed Ansari, the President of the Muslim League, presided over this 42nd Annual Session.

The session was a landmark event in the history of the freedom struggle, as it passed a resolution calling for the boycott of the Simon Commission: a seven-member all-European committee led by Sir John Simon.

In 1928, the Commission was scheduled to visit India to discuss constitutional and political reforms. As the political parties in India were not consulted or invited to participate in the process, the Congress decided to boycott the Simon Commission ‘at every stage and in every form’.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: Who among the following drafted the fundamental rights resolution for the Karachi Session of Congress in 1931?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Option b is correct: Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru drafted the fundamental rights resolution and economic program for the Karachi Session of the Indian National Congress in 1931.

The resolution was adopted by the Congress and became an important milestone in the struggle for India’s independence.

The resolution outlined a number of fundamental rights that the Congress believed should be guaranteed to all Indians.

question:
With reference to the All-India Trade Union Congress (AITUC), consider the following statements:
Statement I: Lala Lajpat Rai became the first President of the All-India Trade Union Congress.
Statement II: The Gaya Session (1922) of the Indian National Congress supported the formation of the All-India Trade Union Congress.

Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (c)

The All-India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) was founded in 1920 in Bombay (now Mumbai). It was founded by leaders including Lala Lajpat Rai, Joseph Baptista, N.M. Joshi and Diwan Chaman Lal.

Statement I is correct: Lala Lajpat Rai was elected as the founding/first President of AITUC at its inaugural session held in 1920 in Bombay. Lala Lajpat Rai was the first to link capitalism with imperialism. The first General Secretary of the AITUC was Dewan Chaman Lal.

Statement II is correct: The Gaya Session of the Indian National Congress (1922), presided over by C. R. Das, welcomed and supported the formation of the AITUC and a committee was formed to assist it.

topic: Civil Disobedience Movement and Round Table
question: With reference to the Indian National Movement, which of the following best describes the Qissa Khwani Bazaar massacre?
options: Firing by British troops on unarmed, non-violent protesters associated with the Khudai Khidmatgar movement at Peshawar.
Use of armed force by British authorities against a women-led tribal movement under Rani Gaidinliu in the Naga Hills.
Opening of fire by British authorities on nationalist crowds following the declaration of martial law at Sholapur in the Bombay Presidency.
Use of military force by the colonial state against revolutionary activists in the aftermath of the Chittagong Armoury Raid in Bengal.
explanation:
Correct Answer: (a)

The Qissa Khwani Bazaar massacre took place on 23 April 1930 at Peshawar in the then North-West Frontier Province during the Civil Disobedience Movement.

The British authorities arrested Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan (Bacha Khan) and other leaders of the Khudai Khidmatgar, popularly known as the Red Shirts, a Pashtun non-violent organization aligned with the Indian National Congress.

In response, a large gathering of unarmed and disciplined non-violent protesters assembled at Qissa Khwani Bazaar, where British Indian Army troops opened fire on the unarmed crowd, with additional use of armored vehicles, resulting in significant casualties.

question:
With reference to revolutionary organisations during the Indian freedom struggle, consider the following freedom fighters:
I. Ram Prasad Bismil
II. Bhagwati Charan Vohra
III. Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee
IV. Chandrashekhar Azad

How many of the above freedom fighters were founders of the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA)?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

The Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) was formed with Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqullah Khan, Sachindra Nath Bakshi and Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee as founding members. Thus, options I and III are correct.

Figures such as Chandra Shekhar Azad, Bhagwati Charan Vohra and Bhagat Singh would also later join the HRA.

Their manifesto, largely penned down by Bismil, was officially released on January 1, 1925 and titled Krantikari (Revolutionary). It proclaimed, “The immediate object of the revolutionary party in the domain of politics is to establish a federal Republic of the United States of India by an organized and armed revolution.”

question:
In the context of Indian freedom struggle, which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the All India Liberal Federation?
Statement I: It was established by Chittaranjan Das as an organized group within the Indian National Congress.
Statement II: It advocated the attainment of swaraj through an extra-constitutional mass struggle against British rule.

Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Correct Answer: (d)

Statement I is incorrect: The All India Liberal Federation was founded by Surendranath Banerjee in 1918 or 1919. It was led by Surendra Nath Banerjea and other prominent leaders were Tej Bahadur Sapru and V. S. Srinivasa Sastri.

Statement II is incorrect: The All India Liberal Federation, also known as the Indian National Liberal Federation (INLF), was aimed to engage in constitutional discussions with the British. Its stated goal was the attainment of responsible government for India through constitutional means. It advocated for gradual constitutional reforms and held a pro-British stance, opposing extra-constitutional mass struggle.

question:
With reference to books and their authors during the Indian freedom movement, consider the following pairs:

Book — Author
I. Bandi Jiwan — Sachindranath Sanyal
II. Pather Dabi — Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay
III. India in Transition — Bhagwati Charan Vohra

How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation:
Correct Answer: (b)

Pair I is correct: Bandi Jivan (A life of Captivity) is the autobiographical three-part account written by Sachindranath Sanyal. Written in 1922, it largely deals with the revolutionary activities of Sachindra Nath over the course of his political career, as well as spells in prison.

Pair II is correct: Pather Dabi was authored by Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay. In 1926, Pather Dabi was published as a novel under Cotton Press, one of the few willing to print his subversive story.

Pair III is incorrect: India in Transition is a political and ideological work authored by M. N. Roy, focusing on India’s socio-economic transformation and revolutionary change. Bhagwati Charan Vohra is best known for his pamphlet/article “The Philosophy of the Bomb”.
